\Section{Introduction}
\label{sec:intro}

The Move Prover (\MVP)~\cite{DillGPQXZ22} is a formal verification tool for Move smart contracts that is designed to be used routinely during code development, with a finish time comparable to type checkers and linters.
\MVP is deployed at Aptos~\cite{ParkZGXGCLC24} for formal verification of core protocol logic such as staking, metering, code deployment, and auxiliary data structures that underpin the framework.
It has been originally developed for the Libra project at Meta, where it was used to specify the Diem payment network.

Recently, the Move language was extended with \emph{higher-order functions} (HO)~\cite{grieskamp2025movehigherorder}.
In \emph{Move 2}, functions are first-class values: they can be passed as arguments, returned from functions, stored in struct fields, and persisted in global storage.
Combined with Move's resource model, persisted function values give the developer a principled account of \emph{dynamic dispatch}.
Typical use cases on Aptos include callback-based asset flows, extensible asset managers, dispatchable permissions and metering, and generic data structure operations parameterized by a caller-provided function (e.g.\ fold, map, filter, custom comparators).

Higher-order functions are a known challenge for formal verification.
They break the modular, static-dispatch specification style on which \MVP was built.
When a function is dispatched dynamically, its pre- and post-conditions cannot in general be looked up at the call site.
Instead, the caller's specification must refer \emph{abstractly} to the behavior of its function-value arguments, and the verifier must reconcile this abstract specification with whichever concrete function happens to reach the call site.
For a production-oriented prover, this reconciliation must be fast, complete in the relevant cases, and free of the trigger instability that higher-order quantification easily introduces~\cite{BUTTERFLY}.

\Paragraph{Contributions}
This paper describes the extension of \MVP to support higher-order Move, and the underlying extension of the Move specification language:
Specifically:

\begin{itemize}
    \item We extend Move specifications with \emph{behavioral predicates}: $\requiresof{f}{\bar{x}}$, $\abortsof{f}{\bar{x}}$, and $\ensuresof{f}{\bar{x}}$, which characterize the pre-/abort-/post-behavior of a function value $f$ on arguments $\bar{x}$, enabling reasoning about function specifications as first-class citizens.

    \item We introduce \emph{state labels} into Move specifications, which name intermediate memory states along a function's execution and connect them to the pre- and post-state via two-state predicates, as in $(\slabeled{..S}{\phi}) \land (\slabeled{S..}{\psi})$.
    This enables reasoning about sequential evolution of global state in predicates.

    \item We describe how \MVP implements invocation of function values on top of SMT~\cite{SMTLib2010,z3}, by discriminating over the possible types of function values flowing into the call site: if $f$ is known (a ground constructor), the invocation is replaced by the effect of the concrete function; if $f$ is not known (for example, a function-typed parameter or a closure loaded from storage), the behavioral predicates of $f$ describe its effect.

    \item We validate the design by extending \MVP's \emph{specification inference} tool to function-valued code.
    With behavioral predicates as the abstraction, the tool computes weakest-preconditions over Move bytecode that contains closures and dynamic dispatch.
    Feeding those generated specs back into the verifier validates completeness and correctness of the solution.
\end{itemize}

The remainder of the paper is organized as follows.
Sec.~\ref{sec:move-hof} reviews Move on Aptos, introduces the running automated-market-maker (AMM) example, and illustrates common specification patterns using function values.
Sec.~\ref{sec:enc} describes the encoding of behavioral predicates and state labels, and describes the Boogie encoding of dynamic dispatch in \MVP.
Sec.~\ref{sec:validation} reports on validating the encoding via the specification inference tool.
Sec.~\ref{sec:conclusion} discusses related work and concludes.
